data.title: Sylogist Has Some Positive Momentum But Still Not Convincing
data.description:
Sylogist Ltd.(OTCPK:SYZLF) is a SaaS provider that offers mission-critical solutions on ERP, CRM, and accounting/payment. It serves 2k+ customers in government, non-profit, and education. In the last 5 years, the stock has not provided good returns for the shareholders, and the price has fluctuated between $6 and $10. As a Canada-based microcap, this business isn't receiving enough coverage, while the business has shown some positive momentum recently. Overall, I do like the business model and recent development. However, more time is needed to prove their market and margin capabilities for me to have long-term conviction.

The business is in a positive momentum during transition

Sylogist's 2024 Q4 earnings call has shown many positive developments. From the surface, the company's sales dipped 1% in Q4 and only grew 0.1% for the full year 2024 (65M). However, the companies' cloud transition has gone well. Saas subscription revenue grew 12.6% for the full year, and Saas NRR (net retention rate) is 108%. Bookings also grew 56% YOY. Moreover, their product perception continued to improve, with NPS growing from 51% in 2023 to 62% in 2024. Product win rates stay high at above 50%. The management is very confident that their cloud offerings will keep upward momentum and guide a Saas ARR growth rate of 20-25%.

One negative element is the added operational costs related to the transition to partner led channel as Sylogist wants to rely on third parties to sale and deliver their softwares. This requires new onboarding and support system for partners which involve more overhead costs. As a results, Adjust EBITA decreased to 16.2M and gross marking also declined 200 basis points to 60%.

Overall, I like what Sylogist is doing. It is clear that the current CEO is very capable and has ambitions and visions to grow the business. Sylogist definitely become a stronger company after him.

Sylogist's competitive advantage is still in question

While recent earnings calls have shown great progress, investors still need to have a strong long-term view to build conviction. On the positive side, I do think Sylogist has some level of competitive advantage. First, it strategically focuses on the mid-market, which includes smaller cities and municipalities. These areas are often not the areas competitors like Tyler Technologies will go to. Second, it was built based on Microsoft Dynamics 365, which may serve better with a large number of clients who also use Microsoft products. Third, their products are known to be customizable and user-friendly as they have been around since 1993.

Although the abovementioned factors did help Sylogist form a business moat, I have doubts about its durability and width. After all, Sylogist's technology isn't 5x better than their competitors, and it's not difficult to replicate. My initial thought on SaaS companies like Sylogist is that their product must be very sticky. However, it might be the opposite. As modern technologies continue to evolve, there are lots of businesses that offer data transferring and cloud switching services. I may argue that the old on-premise software is stickier since you have made big initial investments and your data is bound with hardware. Therefore, Sylogist is not really immune to churn just because they offer ERP or accounting software. If their products are too expensive or not as competent, people still will switch.

Lower margins don't help the narrative

The management has pitched their internal KPIs, such as NPS score, ARRs, bookings, and NRRs, that are all growing at a healthy pace. I do like these developments and agree their business is gaining shares in the mid-market regions (rural and suburban areas). However, the decreasing margins weakened the narrative. Isn't it true that Saas offerings are more automated and should increase gross margins? Sylogist customers often have limited or even declining budgets, and pricing and maintenance costs are often the most important factors in their buying decisions. It is possible that Sylogist intentionally lowers its pricing to gain market share (or other KPIs). If so, is their higher NPS score because of better product features or a cheaper price? I don't think the management has addressed that yet. If we look at a longer view, this is not just one quarter. Sylogist's gross margins have been dropping consistently in the last five years. It will be very concerning if this continues. To have strong conviction, I do want to see gross margins up to prove their pricing power.

Bottom line

Overall, I think Sylogist is a good microcap target for investors to keep an eye on. It has a long track record of profitability and isn't highly followed by the market. Because of the capital-light nature of Saas and its long reputation in the market, the company can deliver very decent cash flow (around 0.2 per share). Before the current CEO, Sylogist often chose to return cash to shareholders as dividends. Now, the company is focused on reinvesting cash back into business growth, which may be a pivotal event for the stock price. The current evaluation isn't high with EV/sales at three under a fast growth expectation of ARR growing 20-25% in 2025. However, I am not sure if this growth will be sustainable or if the margins will be improved. Also, serving the government is a tough business in the current environment, given all the budget cuts and political debates. There are just too many moving parts. Therefore, I am giving a neutral rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMD: This Dip Is A Gift
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

My first article on Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) came out in late August 2023 with a "Sell" rating. I argued at the time that AMD was supposed to fall because of its "over-diversification" and the impact of rising interest rates. Time went, and I eventually upgraded the AMD stock first to "Hold" (December 2023), and then to "Strong Buy" (January 2025), with my latest update stating that AMD it should recover in the non-data-center business units, and it's the prospective EPS growth rates spoke of a substantial level of undervaluation. Unfortunately, since my previous article, the AMD stock slid deeper, currently trading at 58%+ off its all-time high.

Despite that underperformance amid all the recent tariff news and fears, I think AMD is a great beaten-down GARP stock to consider for the long run, as the business growth is unlikely to go anywhere, even if tariffs hit. The undervaluation looks massive given the prospective EPS expansion, and even after applying a discount to those earnings growth projections, most of the trade war and competition risks seem to be priced in. I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating - the prolonged dip looks like a real gift for dip buyers right now.

Why Do I Think So?

My previous article couldn't cover the most recent Q4 results, and it came out before they were released. So, let's just briefly review the recent financials - maybe they were the reason for the stock's dip?

In Q4 2024, AMD's revenue of ~$7.6 billion (+24.16% YoY) beat the consensus by 1.76%, according to Seeking Alpha data, while the quarterly EPS of $1.09 was almost in line with projections (the beat was minimal at ~0.36%). What was important - now over 1/2 of the firm's sales come from the DC segment, which is contributing the majority of sales growth (+69% YoY in Q4), thanks to the "continued adoption of EPYC server CPUs and the rapid ramp-up of the Instinct AI accelerator business." In the server market, they've been gaining share throughout the year, particularly with the launch of its 5th Gen EPYC "Turin" processors.

The company closed the most recent quarter with a 35.5 per cent share of server revenue, marking a 3.7 percentage point increase year-on-year and a 1.6 percentage point rise quarter-on-quarter. Server unit share also rose by 2.0 percentage points year-on-year and 0.9 percentage points quarter-on-quarter.

Source: Fudzilla citing Mercury Research data

While the enterprise adoption keeps gaining momentum, with AMD's EPYC cloud instance usage nearly tripling on a YoY basis in Q4, the company also feels strong demand coming from cloud providers like Amazon's (AMZN) AWS, Google (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), and Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY), who "launched over 100 new AMD-powered instances in Q4 alone."

The AI story for AMD truly took shape last year as they established a multi-billion-dollar Data Center AI franchise, delivering over $5 billion in revenue from its Instinct accelerators. During the earnings call, the management noted that the firm is rapidly expanding deployments of the MI300X GPUs with key partners like Meta (META) - using MI300X exclusively for its Llama 405B model on meta.ai - and Microsoft (powering GPT-4 based Copilot services). At the same time, they've already secured wins with numerous AI-focused Cloud Service Providers ((CSPs)) and enterprise partners like Dell (DELL), who "integrated MI300X into its AI factory solutions".

I still believe that the firm's growth momentum in the DC segment isn't going anywhere, no matter what Trump's and China's new policies bring. As you might have heard, both Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD now face new U.S. government requirements for licenses to export certain advanced AI chips to China (and related entities/countries). For AMD specifically, this impacts their MI308 products.

AMD disclosed in an 8-K filing that this change could result in "charges of up to approximately $800 million related to inventory and purchase commitments":

But compared to Nvidia's disclosure of $5 billion, AMD's China impact seems to be limited for its DC segment. At the same time, we know that the China Semiconductor Industry Association recently suggested that "China's import tariffs might exempt chips from fabless U.S. companies like AMD and Qualcomm (QCOM), whose chips are manufactured by TSMC (TSM) in Taiwan and thus classified as originating from Taiwan", while simultaneously potentially hitting IDMs like Intel (INTC) that manufacture in the U.S. This way, AMD seems to be more-less stably positioned to weather the coming tariff storm, in my understanding.

Anyway, I believe that export controls and tariff noise are creating just short-term headwinds, and all that has already contributed significantly to the recent sell-off in the stock, pricing in all the potential negatives on the business. The potential $800 million charge looks massive, but it seems manageable in the context of AMD's overall business and growth trajectory. This time the diversification should play in hand. The main risk now is the possibility of broader restrictions that could limit access to the large Chinese market in a more radical way, disrupting AMD's supply chains. But nothing idiosyncratic, I mean, nothing specific to AMD in here - if the restrictions come, they likely come for the peers as well.

On the other hand, AMD's key growth drivers - particularly AI and data center dominance outside of China - still remain largely intact, and that's the most important thing to keep in mind, in my opinion. The firm's recent move to utilize the TSMC plant in Arizona seems to be a smart move towards further diversification with a focus on the same DC segment, but on a different niche level.

So while the situation in China looks quite concerning, I don't believe it fundamentally calls into question my long-term bullish thesis, especially given AMD's increasing momentum in the AI and server markets globally.

AMD's pull-in of the next-generation MI350 series sampling and production ramp to mid-2025 should be a massive tailwind to the firm's top line in the next few quarters, improving AMD's competitive positioning against Nvidia's Blackwell platform and also broadening AMD's capabilities (particularly in training large models). In the longer term, the recently closed acquisition of ZT Systems should bring crucial rack-level and system design expertise in-house, thus enabling AMD to offer more complete AI solutions and accelerate time-to-market (especially so for the MI400 generation planned for calendar 2026). So in my view, the current revenue projection that we see in the consensus data may be at least fair, holding an upside in case the current fears of tariffs subside at some point in the future.

The EPS figures are projected to grow at a CAGR of slightly over 20% up to FY2029, and the stock is trading at 14.3x forward P/E (for FY2026). Looking at FY2025, the P/E of 19.16x is already at the lowest point since at least 2018, based on YCharts data:

Let's assume right away that the firm's bottom line is indeed hit hard by tariffs in the next 2 years, and the diversification doesn't help. At a 10% discount to FY2026 EPS estimate (I'm taking a forward multiple here), and only 20x in earnings, the stock's fair value is $110.88/share. That's ~25.58% above today's price. Again, this calculation assumes a very dramatic scenario and a relatively cheap exit valuation multiple. At 25x and in line with the current EPS consensus projection, the implied fair value would be $154, which is 74.42% above today's price.

Given all that, I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating again.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

First off, I can be wrong in my assessment of tariff risks - the $800 million potential charge that AMD's management disclosed may be just the beginning if the trade war escalates from here on. While most of the geopolitical risks seem to be already reflected in AMD's depressed stock price right now, the actual outcome for the future price action is impossible to predict in the current abnormal market environment.

Also, after the Q4 came out, Goldman Sachs reiterated its "Neutral" rating on AMD because of the "near-term deceleration Data Center" segment and lower-than-expected guidance for Q1.

Indeed, AMD's management noted during the earnings call that Q1 revenue should be ~$7.1 billion (+/- $300 million), which is 30% YoY growth but a 7% QoQ decline due to seasonality. Any QoQ decline looks like a bad sign for semiconductor investors when there's a peer like NVDA. But from the same guidance, we know that for Q1, AMD's gross margin should remain stable at ~54%, while for the full year 2025, the management sounded confident in "delivering strong double-digit percentage revenue and EPS growth." So again, most risks seem to be already reflected in the stock price following the heavy YTD correction.

The Bottom Line

For me, the recent weakness of AMD stock amid all the recent tariff news seems to be a real gift. I think investors who think that the firm's rising role in enabling the "AI revolution" and rapidly expanding market shares in different niches, as well as the strength in the DC segment (despite Goldman's contrarian view here), point to a great recovery prospect in the next few years. The current stock price levels already suggest a massive undervaluation. Under quite bearish scenarios, AMD is at least 25% undervalued, while this undervaluation level might be well over 70-75% when the markets stabilize, in my view. So I don't call back my "Strong Buy" for AMD today.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: IDV: Long Term Risk Of Chasing Only Dividend Yield
data.description:
Investment Thesis

iShares International Select Dividend ETF (BATS:IDV) warrants a hold rating due to multiple suboptimal qualities compared to peer international dividend funds. While IDV offers a relatively high dividend yield, multiple peer funds have top holdings with stronger fundamentals leading to greater growth and dividend sustainability. Additionally, IDV has relatively high fees, reducing investor returns over the long term.

iShares International Select Dividend ETF - Overview and Compared Funds

IDV is a passive ETF that seeks to offer investors with high quality international companies that offer income through substantial dividend yields. The fund was created in 2007 and has 101 holdings with $4.35B in AUM. By sector, IDV is heaviest on financials (29.2% weight), followed by utilities (15.8%) and communication (11.0%). I wrote previously on the fund about a year ago and rated the ETF a hold. While the fund has outperformed the S&P 500 since then, IDV still deserves a hold for reasons discussed in this article.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are Vanguard International High Dividend Yield ETF (VYMI), First Trust Dow Jones Global Select Dividend Index Fund (FGD), and Fidelity International High Dividend ETF (FIDI). Each of these funds track different indexes, but generally have the same objective of international diversification with substantial dividends. VYMI seeks to capture international companies that will have above-average dividend yields. I will cover more on how the fund might capture this more effectively than IDV in greater detail later. FGD is a global fund and therefore is the only fund that includes U.S. companies at 12.6% weight. Finally, FIDI seeks to include international holdings that pay and grow their dividends.

IDV ETF: Historic Performance, Expense Ratio, and Dividend Yield

IDV has a 5-year CAGR of 13.20%. While higher than FIDI’s 5-year average return of 12.39%, IDV lags both VYMI and FGD at 15.19% and 15.03%, respectively. All funds have underperformed the S&P 500 Index, which has seen a 5-year compound annual return of about 18%.

In addition to a slightly weaker total return compared to peer funds, IDV has a relatively high expense ratio. At 0.49%, IDV’s fees are higher than both VYMI and FIDI. Importantly, while IDV’s dividend yield is high, it has been declining over the past five years. I will cover more on the reasons for this later.

Expense Ratio, AUM, and Dividend Yield Comparison

IDV

VYMI

FGD

FIDI

Expense Ratio

0.49%

0.17%

0.56%

0.18%

AUM

$4.35B

$9.54B

$583.81M

$108.45M

Dividend Yield TTM

5.61%

4.53%

5.86%

5.25%

Dividend Growth 5 YR CAGR

-4.66%

5.37%

-3.46%

4.28%

Source: Seeking Alpha, 16 Apr 25

IDV Holdings and Key Consideration Factors

IDV has just over 100 holdings and is the most concentrated fund examined, with 27.8% weight on its top 10 holdings. By weight, IDV's top holdings include British American Tobacco (BTI), (LON:BATS), TotalEnergies (TTE), and Enel Chile (ENIC)(MI:ENEL).

Top 10 Holdings for IDV and Compared International ETFs

IDV – 101 holdings

VYMI – 1,561 holdings

FGD – 100 holdings

FIDI – 118 holdings

LON:BATS – 4.23%

SW:NESN – 1.84%

BME:ENG – 1.99%

NESN – 3.06%

TTE – 3.47%

ROG – 1.62%

NZE:SPK – 1.81%

ENEL – 3.02%

ENEL – 2.98%

SHEL – 1.57%

LON:PHNX – 1.79%

GB:NG – 2.86%

GB:NG – 2.90%

NOVN – 1.55%

TSX:BCE – 1.57%

FR:LI – 2.57%

BHP – 2.80%

TYO:7203 – 1.45%

TSX:LIF – 1.40%

SSAB – 2.41%

RIO – 2.63%

HSBA – 1.43%

KSS – 1.37%

CA:NTR – 2.32%

MBG – 2.52%

CBA – 1.13%

TSX:PEY– 1.35%

HK:823 – 2.31%

VOD – 2.13%

RY – 1.12%

LON:LGEN – 1.31%

JP:8963 – 2.30%

ENI – 2.11%

8306 – 1.08%

LON:BATS – 1.29%

BASF – 2.30%

VOW3 – 2.01%

ALV – 1.05%

AMKAF – 1.27%

AU:ORG – 2.27%

Source: Multiple, compiled by author on 16 Apr 25

Looking forward, the long-term returns for IDV will be dependent on the quality of its holdings. However, quality holdings with strong fundamentals typically have low payout ratios that lead to increasing dividend yields. Conversely, holdings with poor fundamental metrics may need higher payout ratios to cover their dividend payouts, increasing the risk of a dividend cut. Unfortunately, IDV's top holdings fall into this second category of higher payout ratios with increased risk as discussed below.

Consideration #1: Holding Quality

The first key consideration is the quality of each fund’s top holdings. IDV advertises that it seeks to capture high-quality international holdings. However, it is important to delineate how “high quality” is defined. If high quality is measured by a high dividend yield, then IDV’s top holding, British American Tobacco, is a strong holding with a dividend yield over 7%. However, a company could be offering a high dividend yield but still lack a solid foundation for long-term quality. This is arguably true for British American Tobacco, which is operating in an environment of declining tobacco demand for all age groups. This decline has contributed to British American Tobacco’s -5.2% YoY revenue growth. While the company may be attractive in the short term and has seen a 48% share price increase over the past year, it has a concerning long-term outlook.

In contrast, top holdings for other funds have demonstrated higher quality. For example, VYMI’s top holding, Nestle (OTCPK:NSRGF)(SW:NESN), a multinational food and drink company, has demonstrated steady, international growth. Unlike British American Tobacco, Nestle saw positive sales growth for both developed and emerging markets last year. VYMI captures other strong holdings as well, including Novartis (NVS), a Swiss pharmaceutical company, with a 10.8% YoY revenue growth and 23.1% net income margin. Therefore, VYMI has an advantage over IDV in terms of multiple top-weighted quality holdings.

Consideration #2: Dividend Payout Ratios

Companies with solid fundamental growth will also be able to maintain sustainable dividend yields. This sustainability can be measured by a company’s dividend payout ratio, which measures the portion of profits paid as a dividend. A high payout ratio could potentially lead to dividend cuts. While British American Tobacco has a 7% dividend yield as previously discussed, the company has a relatively high payout ratio of 71.8%. As a result, the company has been unable to significantly increase its dividend over the past five years. Looking at the average payout ratio for IDV’s top five holdings, we see an average payout ratio higher than VYMI. This implies both lower dividend sustainability and lower capacity for future growth.

IDV

Payout Ratio

VYMI

Payout Ratio

FGD

Payout Ratio

71.81%

TSX:LIF

Average

Source: Compiled by Author from Multiple Sources, 16 Apr 25

On average, VYMI’s payout ratios are lower than the average payout ratio for IDV's top five holdings. While holdings like Toyota (TYO:7203) may have a lower yield, its lower payout ratio of 22.2% contributes to greater dividend growth as measured by a 3-year CAGR of 6.3%. As a result, VYMI demonstrates increased long-term dividend sustainability compared to IDV.

Consideration #3: Declining Dividend Yields

While high payout ratios do not always lead to declining dividend yields, we can plot each funds’ dividend history to see this trend. Both IDV and FGD had average payout ratios of over 50% for its top five holdings. This average payout ratio has led to the overall declining yield for IDV, with a -4.66% 5-year dividend growth CAGR.

The lower average payout ratio for VYMI, as previously discussed, has contributed to a 5-year dividend growth of 5.37% CAGR. Therefore, while a low payout ratio does not always lead to increasing dividend yields, there is a correlation of these qualities. As a result, VYMI appears much better postured to grow its dividend over the long term compared to IDV.

Current Valuation

IDV has done considerably better than peer funds and the U.S. market, over the past year with a price return of over 14%. This performance is largely driven by investors seeking global diversification and dividend yields due to recent volatility.

Even with a strong share price performance over the past year, IDV has the most attractive valuation as measured by its price-to-earnings ratio. Its performance will likely continue to be strong as long as volatility in the U.S. market persists. Additionally, an increased risk of recession will likely continue driving investors towards income-producing dividend funds. Despite these factors, IDV’s valuation alone does not drive a buy rating. The fundamental qualities of its top holdings, including high payout ratios, are reason to suspect low to negative dividend yield growth in coming years.

Valuation Metrics for IDV and Peer Competitors

IDV

VYMI

FGD

FIDI

P/E ratio

8.97

11.70

9.24

12.79

P/B ratio

0.86

1.40

0.81

1.31

Source: Compiled by Author from Each Fund’s Website, 16 Apr 25

Risks to Investors

Due to its lack of U.S. holdings, IDV primary risk factor is the opportunity cost of investing internationally compared to U.S. domestic investment. As depicted by Hartford Funds, US equities have seen a period of outperformance lasting about 14 years. Should this trend reverse, as seen in previous historic periods, international funds like IDV could see a strong boost.

On the other hand, U.S. equities could continue to see superior performance for years to come. In the short term, a key hurdle will be the 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs which will expire in July. If the ongoing trade war can be resolved, recession and inflationary risks could ease, presenting a strong opportunity for continued U.S. equity outperformance.

Concluding Summary

While IDV has seen strong recent performance, the fund does not deserve a buy rating due to the fundamental metrics of its top companies. These metrics, including relatively high dividend payout ratios, point towards a slow decline of the fund’s dividend yield and low growth. Additionally, IDV has relatively high fees. By comparison, VYMI was the strongest international dividend fund analyzed due to its combination of growth, dividend sustainability, and low fees.

data.title: Credo Technology: The Stars Are Aligned For A Bullish Reversal (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (NASDAQ:CRDO) has been crushed since its January high, with losses of over 50% since then. I believe this selloff is unjustified and now creates a major buying opportunity. In the below analysis, while many technicals remain bearish, the recent bullish engulfing pattern along with positive divergence in key indicators makes the case for a technical bottom. For the fundamentals, most recent earnings were strong and Q4 guidance was also highly impressive. Revenue growth is expected to continue accelerating, indicating a booming business. However, the P/S ratio has recently shown major contraction and has detached from the fundamentals of the company. While the premium versus the IT sector remains high, its strong growth prospects and its innovative connectivity solutions are able to justify a high valuation. Therefore, I initiate Credo at a buy rating, with the technicals showing a bottom and the fundamentals showing that the selloff has been irrational.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

Credo remains in a downtrend that began in early February. The downtrend line has been a key source of resistance lately and may continue to pressure the stock. On the bright side, there has been an emergence of a bullish engulfing pattern lately that may signal that the bottom is in. The formation is right at the 30 level and so that area could be support moving forward. There are, however, three significant resistance levels above the stock currently. The nearest one is at about 39. This level was key support in mid-November and in early March. Moving up, the mid-40s is likely to be resistance as well, with two peaks this month at this level. Lastly, the 50 price level is a key round number that acted as resistance in late March. Note that the early December upside gap also occurred at this level.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50-day SMA is still above the 200-day SMA and has been for the past six months. While this is a positive long-term sign, the gap between these SMAs has been shrinking since mid-February. A death cross may now be nearly unavoidable as the lines converge. In addition, the stock broke below the 200-day SMA back in late March as a major sign of weakness. For the near term, the latest crossover between the 9 day and 21 day EMAs was back in February, and it was a bearish one. No crossovers since then shows persistent bearishness in the stock. The deviation between the EMAs has closed a bit recently and so, at least, near term bearish momentum seems to be slowing.

The stock broke below the Bollinger Bands midline back in late February as a signal that a downtrend could be forming. The midline then went on to confirm the downtrend by acting as resistance multiple times since then. The stock just recently experienced resistance there this month. For the MACD, there was a bullish crossover not long ago, but the indicator hasn't really been able to pull away from the signal line. This histogram has been quite flat, and so there seems to be resilient bears in the stock. There is positive divergence in the MACD, however, as the indicator made a higher low during the stock's recent six-month low. For the RSI, it currently has a reading of 41.61 after recovering from a near 30 level earlier this month. Bearish control has therefore decreased as of late. Similar to the MACD, the RSI shows bullish divergence to a lesser degree, as the April low in the indicator seems to have held a hair above its March trough. Lastly, for the stochastics, there was a bullish crossover earlier this month and recently, the %K was able to bounce off the %D successfully as a sign of strong bulls. Both lines are also now back above 50 to show net bullish control in the stock. Like the MACD and RSI, the stochastics also shows positive divergence.

Takeaway

In my view, while the trend is still severely downward at this point, the technical bottom for Credo may now be in. The downtrend line and weakening SMAs may be concerning but a stock reversal may now be in the cards. The emergence of a bullish engulfing pattern along with positive divergence signals in the three key indicators discussed above show that there is a high possibility that a bullish reversal may be starting and that the stock may see gains from here.

Fibonacci

Retracement

Using the January high and the recent six-month low as the inputs for the Fibonacci Retracement, key targets are provided in the chart above. As you can see, recently the 23.6% level acted as resistance as the stock was unable to push above the low 40s price range. This level could be resistance again if the stock does continue its bullish reversal. If the stock breaks above this area, the next level at 38.2% could be another significant one, as it's very near the 50 round number. Recall, that the 50 level was identified as a resistance level in the above analysis as well. With the early December gap up and late March peak at this level, the stock may experience difficulty in this area.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

Credo stock remains a significant distance below the cloud and so general bearishness is present. The cloud is also now bearish as the Leading Span A broke beneath the Leading Span B at the start of April. Moving into May, the cloud itself is displaying worrying signs. Firstly, the cloud has moved significantly downward and secondly, the thickness of the cloud has increased to a great extent. Therefore, the stock's bullish reversal could be put to the test moving forward. For the near term, the Conversion Line remains below the Base Line after a bearish crossover back in late February. However, the deviation between these two lines has decreased substantially recently and so bearish momentum has been slowing for the near term. Lastly, the Lagging Span is currently very near the stock price of early March but remains slightly below. This weakly confirms the downtrend as clearly downward momentum has declined for Credo. Overall, I would say the Ichimoku Cloud shows that the near term has definitely improved for the stock, but the most worrying sign here is that the cloud itself may be a potential obstacle as we head into May.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Credo reported their FY2025 Q3 earnings in early March and I would say they showed strong results overall. The company generated revenues of $135 million, up 154.4% YoY and 87.4% sequentially. As you can see in the above chart, revenue growth for this quarter was the second highest of the past three years and has been on a nice uptrend since FY2024 Q1. For EPS, they reported an adjusted figure of $0.25, up 525% YoY and 257% QoQ. Both revenues and EPS surpassed analyst estimates as they beat by $14.64 million and $0.07 respectively, showing strong performance. Also shown in the above chart is their cash and short-term investments balance. At the end of their Q3, they had $379.21 million. With their cash provided from operations and cash provided from investing activities both being positive for the quarter, the company is on very strong financial footing at this point. Their investments in marketable and equity securities brought in an impressive $63.7 million in cash. Lastly, margins also saw improvement. Adjusted gross margin increased from 62.2% to 63.8% YoY while adjusted operating margin surged to 31.4% from only 4.6%. The efficiency of their business is therefore on the right track. As a whole, there is not much to complain about in their Q3 results as financial metrics showed healthy improvement YoY and sequentially as well.

Guidance

For their FY2025 Q4, Credo is expecting to generate revenues of between $155 million and $165 million. At the midpoint this would be a 163% YoY increase from $60.8 million in FY2024 Q4. This is therefore an acceleration of growth versus Q3's 154.4% growth rate. They also provided guidance for their gross margin. Credo is expecting an adjusted figure of between 63% and 65%, a minor decrease from the prior year period's 66.1%. Overall, while modest declines in gross margin may be slightly concerning, the strong revenue guidance shows that the company is in for another impressive quarter. In addition, Seeking Alpha has an A+ Revision Grade for Credo as of this writing as there have been 10 FY1 Up Revisions versus no FY1 Down Revisions in the past 90 days. Clearly, the outlook remains strong for the company.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio has contracted significantly from the high set earlier this year. After nearing 60, the multiple is back to a reading of only 19.41 now. While the highs earlier in the year may have been a bit extreme, I believe the contraction in the P/S ratio has been severely overdone. As I discussed above, revenue growth for their Q3 was the second highest in the past three years as the uptrend in the figure continues. Furthermore, Q4 guidance shows that revenue growth is expected to accelerate and so the outlook is very bright at this point. While the increased revenues, of course, contributed to the P/S contraction, the market now seems to be undervaluing the growth prospects of Credo. The multiple contraction in the P/S is overdone, as the around average level in the ratio is disconnected with the accelerating growth projections. That said, the IT sector currently only has a median P/S ratio of 2.57 and so the stock is at a major premium. Given that the company is a major innovator in connectivity solutions and growth is still accelerating (a rare feat in this environment), this valuation is not unjustified in my view. In addition, at the time of this writing, Seeking Alpha has put Credo at the top of the Semiconductors Industry for their Quant Ranking.

Conclusion

The stars seem to be aligning for Credo stock. For the technicals, clear bottoming signs are beginning to appear in the form of bullish divergence and a bullish engulfing pattern. For the fundamentals, Q3 earnings were strong and Q4 guidance was impressive as strong growth acceleration is hard to come by in this tough macro-environment. As for the valuation, the P/S ratio has contracted significantly since the peak earlier in the year to around average levels currently. This is a detachment from the increasingly strong and bright outlook for the company and suggests undervaluation. While the premium to the IT sector may seem daunting, Credo's strong financial growth and innovative technologies can justify its high valuation. Therefore, I initiate Credo at a buy rating as the technicals and fundamentals are aligned in their bullish setups for the stock.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Neutral Sentiment Grows
data.description:
Neutral sentiment among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and pessimism decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 3.1 percentage points to 25.4%. Bullish sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 37.5% for the 14th time in 16 weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 5.2 percentage points to 17.7%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 39th time in 41 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 2.1 percentage points to 56.9%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 20th time in 22 weeks. Bearish sentiment has now been above 50% for eight consecutive weeks, the longest period over 50% in the survey's history.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 1.0 percentage points to –31.4%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 15th time in 17 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members what types of stocks they are favoring.

Here is how they responded:

  • Dividend stocks: 35.5%
  • Value stocks: 15.8%
  • Growth stocks: 9.6%
  • Small-cap stocks: 2.5%
  • A mix of the above/other: 36.3%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 25.4%, down 3.1 percentage points
  • Neutral: 17.7%, up 5.2 percentage points
  • Bearish: 56.9%, down 2.1 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: Bank OZK: Still A Steal Post-Q1 Results
data.description:
In a quasi-case of Groundhog Day, shares of Bank OZK (NASDAQ:OZK) have been struggling lately, falling by around 11% since my last piece in December. I say a quasi-case: OZK has actually outperformed regional bank ETFs in that time, albeit it has had a harder fall from its year-to-date peak in February.

Followers of OZK will not be surprised. This Arkansas-based lender writes a relatively large amount of construction loans, and given recent events, well, it wouldn't be hard to craft a downbeat narrative for OZK compared to the average bank.

I continue to remain much more bullish. For one, OZK has more than proved its spurs over the years in terms of credit quality. Not just that, but the stock now trades for less than 7x forward EPS, affording investors a very large margin of safety. Given first quarter results look fine to me, I'm leaving my 'Strong Buy' rating for OZK in place.

Q1 2025 Review

In the first quarter, OZK earned $167.9 million in net income, or $1.47 per share. The latter was down 2.6% year-on-year and 5.8% sequentially, but still mapped to a stellar 1.77% return on average assets.

Net interest income was relatively flat at $374.5 million, with OZK's net interest margin declining 2bps sequentially to 4.31%. OZK's loan book is largely variable rate, and loans account for the vast majority of its interest-earning assets. Following last year's rate cuts, it has simply lost a bit more on the asset side than it has gained from cheaper funding.

Positively, NIM contraction is moderating. Because OZK doesn't source particularly cheap funding, then if interest rates hold steady, it should benefit quite nicely from the repricing of its more market-like liabilities (like time deposits, for example). This should lead to NIM expansion later in the year.

Operating costs rose ~10% year-on-year to $147 million, exceeding revenue growth. Despite this, OZK remains an incredibly efficient bank. Expressed relative to assets, it spends much less than the average peer does on operating costs. This is also true once you strip out non-interest income as well.

Underwriting is really the strongest aspect of Bank OZK. In short, it writes relatively high-yielding loans while suffering very few loan losses. Over the past seventeen years, OZK's average gross loan yield was over 100bps higher than the peer average, while its average net charge-off ratio was around 30bps below the average.

Looking at its balance sheet, loans account for a higher share of OZK's assets than the average bank. Keeping losses contained is therefore relatively more important in terms of its profitability. Alongside the nature of its lending (more commercial real estate, particularly construction), this could also explain why the market tends to punish OZK more during periods of economic uncertainty.

With the above in mind, you could also argue that monitoring credit quality is more important here than for other lenders. So far, things still look pretty good on that front. Net charge-offs did increase 5bps compared to FY2024, but at 25bps, losses remain low. Only $45 million of OZK's loans are past due, equal to just 0.14% of total lending. Nonperforming assets were around 0.55%, roughly flat quarter-on-quarter. This is mostly foreclosed real estate that OZK can claw back value from. Plus, its ratio of NPAs to total assets is also not worse than other banks.

Given the recent bout of uncertainty, it is understandable that investors may be worried about credit risk. OZK has several lines of defense here. Should defaults rise, well, its average loan-to-cost and loan-to-value ratios were only 45% last quarter in its RESG division. That affords it a pretty solid collateral cushion. Furthermore, OZK is generating a very strong ~2.75% pre-provision ROA. That is much, much higher than the average bank, meaning OZK simply has more scope to absorb credit charges. It has a $639 million credit loss allowance, equal to 1.28% of loans and unfunded commitments. Relative to actual NPAs, this is equal to a strong ~3x cover. Thirdly, OZK has ample capital. Its ratio of tangible common equity to tangible assets was 12.55% at the end of last quarter. While we obviously don't have comprehensive peer data yet, that will be well above average.

The Valuation

OZK stock trades for $41.11 as I type, equal to a little under tangible book value per share. This is a bank that earned a strong 14.3% Return on Average Tangible Common Equity [ROATCE] last quarter, meaning its P/E ratio is well into the single-digits. OZK could earn a 10% ROATCE and still trade for less than 10x earnings. Looking at its actual historical ROATCE (around 15% over the past decade), that seems conservative to say the least.

Given its strong earnings and modest dividend payout ratio, OZK has continued to compound its TBVPS at a strong clip. Last quarter, this line rose nearly 13% year-on-year to $42.48. As you can see below, that is pretty much right in line with its historical rate. Looking at consensus earnings estimates, OZK should continue to grow its balance sheet at a double-digit annualized clip in the future as well.

Finally, OZK's dividend now yields around 4.4%, and that is on a payout ratio of just 25% of net income. Between balance sheet growth, P/TBVPS expansion and a nice dividend yield, the stock continues to offer investors excellent medium-term total returns potential.

Conclusion

There wasn't much in Bank OZK's first quarter results that caused me concern. NII and margin dynamics were already telegraphed beforehand, and besides, the bank is still earning a stellar ROA. OZK being OZK, credit quality is always going to be in the spotlight: such is the nature of its business model. Positively, things still look very good on that front.

As for the stock, while multiple expansion may well require a shift in economic sentiment to occur, a compelling combination of growth and yield can still drive very good returns either way. On that basis, I am leaving my 'Strong Buy' rating in place.

data.title: JPMorgan: Well-Positioned To Withstand Any 'Kerfuffle'
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last article on JPMorgan Chase & Co. (NYSE:JPM), published in January 2025, I dissected the company’s fourth quarter performance and analysed the long-term impact of the company’s ambitious plan of expanding its consumer business into Europe. I had a HOLD rating on the stock.

Since my article was published, JPM has declined 10.7%, in line with the S&P 500’s decline, which was 11.3% during the same period.

In this article, I analyse the company’s first quarter earnings report and examine whether JPM has the necessary tools to withstand a potential recession, especially since the odds of one has increased in recent week.

First Quarter Highlights

JPM had a very strong start to the year, with a beat on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. Q1 revenues came in at $45.31 billion, up 8.05% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $1.21 billion. Diluted EPS came in at $5.07, up 14.2% y/y and comfortably beating analyst estimates by $0.43. The bank’s book value per share came in at $119.24, up 12% y/y, and tangible book value per share came in at $100.36, up 13% y/y. Net interest income, a key metrics for the bank, came in at $22.6 billion. Provisions for credit losses rose slightly, coming in at $3.31 billion, and missed analyst estimates of $2.70 billion. Return on equity for the first quarter came in at 18%, comfortably beating analyst estimates of 16.2%, and return on tangible equity (ROTCE) came in at 21%, the same as the previous quarter.

The company reaffirmed its 2025 guidance for net interest income (excl. markets), expected to be approximately $90 billion. The bank now expects overall net interest income to come in at approximately $94.5 billion, a slight uptick from the previous guidance of $94 billion, which was also what the consensus estimate was.

JPM Demonstrates Resilience Despite The Tariff-Induced “Kerfuffle”

I enjoy JPM CEO Jamie Dimon’s interviews and commentary, especially given his penchant for colourful ways to describe the economy, policies and regulation amongst other things. During the first quarter earnings call, he described the trade war induced panic that was seen in the Treasury markets as a ‘kerfuffle.’

There is, however, no evidence of any ‘kerfuffle’ in JPM itself if Q1 results are to go by, despite the macroeconomic uncertainties becoming challenging with each passing day. Every single segment of the company performed remarkably well, despite the uncertainty. What was striking to me was the growth seen in the Consumer & Community Banking (CCB) Division. The y/y growth may have only been 3.7%, but it is a remarkable jump compared to the previous quarters. For instance, the growth stood at 1.5% y/y in Q4 of FY24, -3.1% y/y in the third quarter, and 2.7% y/y in the second quarter. Given that the macroenvironment was even more challenging in the quarter gone by, compared to the previous quarter, the fact that JPM was able to achieve this growth, that too with a high base effect, demonstrates the company’s resilience and proves why investors should look at this name, especially during turbulent times like what we are facing now. The bank also announced that US consumer continued to be resilient despite falling sentiment, as reflected by the 7% y/y increase in debit and credit card sales volume. The growth was a lot higher than its consumer-focused peer, BAC, which saw credit- and debit-card spending rising 4% y/y. JPM, however, did warn that the higher growth could be due to consumers “front-loading” their spending ahead of a potential increase in prices due to tariffs. Nonetheless, the CCB division’s growth and performance is a testament to the bank’s ability to weather a challenging macroenvironment in my opinion.

There was also continued strength seen in both the bank’s Corporate & Investment Banking [cib] Division and its Asset & Wealth Management (AWM) Division, both of which, registered a second consecutive quarter of double-digit growth, although growth across both segments did show a decline. On a sequential basis, however, the CIB division registered a double-digit growth for the first time in five quarters. The bank also saw a 12% y/y jump in investment banking fees and maintained its number 1 ranking with a wallet share of 9%. The bank was also able to take full advantage of the increased volatility seen in equity markets ever since President Trump came into the White House, as demonstrated by a 48% y/y jump in equity trading revenue.

Overall, if there’s one key takeaway from JPM’s first quarter, it is that the bank continues to be resilient to the challenging macroenvironment. Despite an elevated net charge-off rate, the bank was still able to demonstrate impressive growth in the CCB division, and both the CIB and AWM divisions continued to be strong catalysts. What is even more impressive is that the bank has already made substantial provisions for credit losses, as the threat of a recession continues to remain elevated albeit lower than during the height of the tariff wars. This means that the bank, in my opinion, remains in a much better position should the recession fears materialize.

Furthermore, President Trump does seem to listen to Jamie Dimon, as evidenced by his recent admission of Mr. Dimon playing a role in his decision to put a 90-day pause on his reciprocal tariffs. Should this be the case for the future, then based on the Mr. Dimon's commentary on the need for more deregulation, the odds of this happening have certainly increased in my opinion. Should this materialize, then it would be an additional tailwind to JPM’s growth. Today, in my opinion, investors need to be looking out for high-quality names to take cover as the macroenvironment becomes more and more uncertain. Based on its Q1 performance and the outlook, JPM certainly fits the bill.

Valuation

Forward P/B Approach

Price Target

$271.00

Projected Forward P/B Multiple

1.83

Projected FY25 Book Value Per Share

$148.15

Source: LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv) and Author’s Calculations

According to LSEG Data, the company currently trades at a forward P/B of 1.83, making it the most expensive among its peers. It is also more expensive today relative to its historical levels, with its historical forward P/B median multiple being 1.5x.

In my last article on JPM, I made my case on why JPM deserves a re-rating of its multiple, given the continued strength and resilience across all its segments. Despite tariff-related uncertainties, and despite the bank’s IB division expected to remain under pressure at least until there’s clarity on the current Administration’s tariff policies, the bank still re-affirmed its FY25 guidance. Furthermore, given that it is a more well-rounded bank compared to some of its peers such as GS and MS, it deserves a multiple higher than them. As such, I have assumed a forward P/B multiple of 1.83x for my calculations, slightly lower than my previous estimate of 2.03x.

JPM’s book value of equity at the end of the first quarter stood at $351.42 billion. The company re-affirmed its NII guidance at $94 billion, which to me is a reasonable estimate even after accounting for the tariff-related uncertainties. After accounting for a tax rate of 20.45%, the NII after taxes come in at $74.78 billion. The company announced a 12% y/y increase in dividends for the first quarter, which is in line with its 10-year dividend growth rate of 11.42%. Assuming the company maintains a 12% y/y dividend growth and assuming the total number of shares outstanding at 2.78 billion, the total dividends for FY25 are expected to come in $14.32 billion. Based on the same number of shares outstanding, the projected book value per share for FY25 is then projected to come in at $148.15 [(351.42 + 74.78 – 14.32)/2.78], representing a y/y growth of about 28% y/y.

A forward P/B of 1.83 and a projected FY25 book value of share of $148.15 results in a price target of $271, which represents an upside of about 18% from current levels. My price target is higher than my previous one ($217), since I calculated my estimate of the bank’s book value per share rather than relying on the company’s track record of historical surprise to offer a more appropriate estimate.

Furthermore, despite the recent selloff, JPM continues to enjoy strong overall momentum, reflected by the B+ grade on Seeking Alpha’s Momentum Scale. Even its short-term momentum stands at a respectable B-. Given the considerable upside from current levels and given the strong momentum seen in the stock, I am upgrading my rating on the stock from a HOLD to a BUY.

Risk Factors

The main risk factor to my bull case is that the recession does arrive a lot earlier than expected and more importantly, it stays for longer. JPM’s own economists, after all, themselves raised the probability of a 2025 recession to 60% from 40%. While there is no doubt that JPM’s balance sheet remains strong, it still would take a hit given that it would have to substantially increase its provision for credit losses, given the increasing delinquencies that would come with the recession. The credit card net charge-off rate has been steadily increasing in the last three quarters, albeit the growth has been marginal. In a recessionary environment, the growth rate would no longer be marginal, which would have a significant negative impact on the bank’s bottom-line.

The second risk factor to my bull case is if the M&A environment remains subdued for a lot longer than initially anticipated. There was a lot of anticipation that deal-making environment would get a boost under a new Administration, but so far, this is yet to materialize. The expectation now is that deal activity picks up in the second half of FY25 once the environment becomes a lot more deregulatory in nature. This is far from guaranteed, given that for now, the FTC has announced it would continue to maintain the Biden-era merger guidelines. JPM’s management, during the earnings call, also cited the need for the uncertainty to be substantially reduced before deal activity can pick up. As such, if uncertainties do remain elevated, then the Investment Banking division of the bank would be negatively affected.

Concluding Thoughts

It was yet another strong quarterly performance by JPM, with the banking giant comfortably beating both the top- and bottom-line estimates. Despite a challenging macroenvironment, the bank’s CCB division demonstrated strong resilience. Furthermore, both the CIB and AWM divisions registered yet another quarter of strong double-digit growth. Overall, it was an all-round performance from JPM and the fact that the bank re-affirmed its guidance despite increasing its provisions for loan losses shows that it has the capability to withstand the recessionary storm, should we get one.

From a valuation perspective, the recent selloff seen in the stock has made it attractive, and as such, I am upgrading my rating on the stock from a HOLD to a BUY. There might be “hurricanes” and “kerfuffles” occurring in the US now as the trade war with China rages on. Based on current evidence, however, JPM remains immune to them.

data.title: Lynas Rare Earths: A Cyclical Play
data.description:
Lynas Rare Earths Limited (OTCPK:LYSCF)/(OTCPK:LYSDY) is a unique opportunity to ride a cyclical wave driven by China's tightening of rare earth minerals being supplied to the U.S. are part of a broader response to the tariffs of the Trump Administration.

I believe Lynas is uniquely positioned to benefit from rare earth price increases from this constrained supply, with a balance sheet that doesn't create a lot of risk if commodity prices turn sour. For that reason, I think it's a solid Buy.

Lynas's Position in Rare Earths

The way Lynas has positioned itself is unique among non-Chinese companies. As the PBS report I linked in the intro mentioned, 70% of America's rare earth minerals are imported from China. A supply constraint of this magnitude necessarily puts upward pressure on these commodity prices. Speaking in the Q2 FY 2025 earnings call, CEO Amanda Lacaze:

The great control of feedstock, generally, we would believe favors Lynas as we are a proven separator with proven resources, and we remain the only non-Chinese producer who can bank the full value of any improvements in the oxide prices.

Rare earths are technically in many places, but the tricky part is having the facilities already set up to mine and process those minerals.

Lynas already has such three such sites, two in Australia (their home country), and one in Malaysia.

They aren't a speculative rare earths project, with annual production of 12K to 15K tonnes of minerals in recent years. As such, they are a viable alternative if Chinese sources tighten.

Financial History and Condition

One may note that the image I just shared indicates that, while volume didn't shift too much over the years, total revenues did, as their average selling price shot up along these cyclical lines. Cost of sales were also relatively stable, allowing Lynas to capture this upside.

Taking a broader view, we see how favorable prices showcase the kind of operating leverage that Lynas has. (Seeking Alpha data in USD.)

Looking at it from a cash flow perspective, we cash from operation skyrockets in these scenarios. Much of this was spent on capex to build out the very capacity that was mentioned before.

Above, they reported that they ended the quarter with $308M in cash. This gives substantial coverage against their debt position.

Between the short-term and long-term borrowings, this sufficiently covers their total debt of $171M. According to their latest quarterly report, this all comes from a loan facility that matures in June 2030, indicating that they have a lot of flexibility on the balance sheet that minimizes much of the risk in case cycles don't favor them.

The Buy Case

Ultimately, this idea is a very simple cyclical play. When prices are up, Lynas benefits, and while recent operating cash flows are in tens of millions per year (in USD), their operating leverage jumps this up to several multiples. As this would increase earnings, there's the moment for shareholders to benefit.

Shares peaked near $8 when commodity prices were up and boosted their revenues. With more capacity and potentially a much sharper impact to rare earth markets ahead, I think there's room for a similar double in the foreseeable future.

There are some risks that are worth highlighting, however:

  • Trump and China backing down
  • Potential moves against Australia
  • Currency exchange risks
  • OTC trading

The first is simple. How long Trump's tariff moves will last (whether they are policy or a tactic in a long-term negotiation) is uncertain. If Trump and China and back down and return to normalcy, which could technically happen at any time, that takes away the supply shortage that would increase prices of rare earths.

In a similar vein, China is not the only target of tariffs, and the same could happen to Australia. If sale of minerals to the U.S. were limited or hindered, this also hurts the thesis.

Even if those issues don't present themselves, the company discloses that some risk exists in relation to the currency of all three countries (see exchange losses recorded above). While most readers will be directly affected by the relationship between the USD and the AUD, even the Chinese Renminbi indirectly matters because of China's influence on mineral prices, and so exchange rates among all three are key.

Lastly, as this is a foreign stock, it trades OTC in the U.S. With a cyclical play like this, it helps to have an exit plan, as OTC stocks can be constrained by insufficient liquidity. American investors should figure out their exit plan before buying into this company.

Conclusion

It's not complicated. If China closes the lid on rare earths, even just for spite, American customers have to look elsewhere. As it stands, Lynas is the lone, reasonable alternative that is completely outside of China. While there are external factors that could confound this, internally Lynas has made all the right moves in preparing its capacity for such a moment.

With the history of its positive results when commodity prices favor them and the impact on the stock price, I think it's easy to see what happens as China's policy on restricting rare earths goes into place and constrains supply. For that reason, I feel comfortable rating this stock a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CEFS: A 'Non-Discounted' Take On Discounted CEFs (In A Bear Market)
data.description:
I've worked with several funds, and arbitrage is always a hot topic, often framed negatively, almost like an elite strategy that has become less and less accessible. Personally, I don't think that's true, it's just about knowing where to look. According to Boaz Weinstein, the creator of the Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (BATS:CEFS), closed-end funds "are one of the last places where inefficiencies still exist."

CEFS is a well-known fund, both for its arbitrage strategies and, in part, for its hefty expense ratio of 4.29%, a number that sounds scary just to write. And yet, experience has taught me that costs should only be evaluated after examining the other aspects of the fund. So that's exactly what I'll do here.

But first: CEFS, here are the fund features you need to know

I'll start with the strategy, definitely the most interesting aspect, as it's an actively managed fund of funds offered as an ETF. CEFS buys CEFs (excuse the wordplay) with large NAV discounts, aiming for the discount to shrink. Then they pressure the manager through activist strategies, with the goal of closing the discount. And this is theoretically CEFS's competitive advantage, and this premium over the market should (theoretically) justify such a high management fee.

As of today: According to the prospectus, at least 80% of the capital should be invested in CEFs, although it maintains wide freedom of choice, including derivatives, shorts, and leverage. And that's exactly the case: it holds about 97 CEFs in the portfolio, with a weighted average discount of 8-9%, up to 6% leverage, and performs active rate hedging.

Currently, it holds activist positions in ECAT (BlackRock ESG Capital Allocation Trust), with over 12% of NAV; and second BTZ (BlackRock Tech & Private Equity), nearly 9%. The first has a NAV discount of 5%, the second a NAV discount of 6.12%.

This leads to an annualized yield of 7.72%, a 30-day SEC yield of 9.30%, distributed in part monthly.

The flexibility is also seen in "unusual" holdings: such as ASA (over 7%), gold and precious metals, like PDI (4.7%) for the income section, and GDV (5.3%) for the dividend section. Small details, but they highlight not only a focus on NAV discount strategies, but also a certain diversification. Let's not forget that this is a fund of funds, this should (at least theoretically) level out standard deviation, shifting the attention to the income side of CEFs.

But does this hold up in reality?

Am I being too simplistic if I answer this by comparing it to the S&P 500? Maybe a bit, but I don't know of any other actively managed ETFs that follow an activist strategy to compare it with. So, I'll start by saying that over the last 5 years, it's shown a very competitive performance compared to the S&P 500, with a standard deviation of 11.29, which is lower. That translates into a better Sharpe ratio.

Its total return is therefore very correlated with U.S. equities, which in my view makes CEFS not a diversifying tool for a portfolio, even if it's often associated with that role. In this regard, the comparisons made by Boaz Weinstein himself, like here, are always interesting: he uses other funds as benchmarks, showing that while CEFS is more expensive, it also tends to outperform, and that activism is complex, but brings decisive alpha for this category.

In this sense, I find this table very useful, as it shows how CEFS, a fund characterized by activism and higher costs, has indeed outperformed its peers focused only on NAV arbitrage.

How to approach these funds

There's no rule here, rather, a personal opinion. One element I personally value a lot in managing my finances (and one that often causes disagreements with colleagues when discussing portfolio management) is a broad use of DCA, especially during a low phase like the current one.

When it comes to income funds, for me, there's no strategy that can compete with a long-term DCA implementation based on a minimum signal (30 points) on the 14-period RSI on a weekly timeframe, combined with dividend reinvestment.

This would generate entry points like those summarized in this simulation, I created using a program I built to send me the right alerts.

Risk

I could keep it simple and say that CEFS shares the same risks as any other U.S. stock, given its correlation; that would be graphically accurate, but not thorough, especially for a fund with such high fees that remains competitive likely because it works well with NAV discount arbitrage.

What widens when trader anxiety or market tension rises? The so-called "risk wrong way." This is often associated with bear markets, but I personally prefer to link it to phases of high uncertainty (technically, zones with a high VIX). When uncertainty rises, the gap between NAV and market price could potentially widen due to typical supply and demand mechanisms. In this sense, panic selling becomes particularly harmful, an eventuality whose probability increases when isolating cases where the VIX is above 30 points, like now.

Given that CEFS has 80% exposure to CEFs and maintains 6% leverage, the potential risk could exceed that of the S&P 500. I say "potential" because active management (theoretically) should also be able to cushion losses better than the S&P 500, which is a static index in this regard.

In fact, in my experience, it's precisely in those moments when the tail end of the VIX spikes to extremes that you find out whether a fund like this is well managed, just one wrong call can create a performance gap against the broader market that becomes virtually irrecoverable.

Conclusion

Following the core strategy of DCA combined with dividend reinvestment, we're currently in a low zone, one that could continue to decline, because mathematical precision and statistical certainty simply don't exist in markets. However, considering that this fund has historically demonstrated excellent management, I don't see any technical reason not to remain positive on CEFS.

Of course, the "risk wrong way" is currently elevated, so I believe it's important to factor in that the probability of further declines is higher than in previous RSI lows. That said, the whole logic behind DCA is precisely to protect against such risks.

data.title: Gold, Guns, And The Market: What Rising Tensions Really Mean For Your Portfolio
data.description:
I’ve been bullish on gold for over a year now. That doesn’t make me a gold bug. I’m not waving a “repent now” placard on a street corner, and I’m certainly not forecasting mushroom clouds and Mad Max. I’m an investor—an ex-trader—who believes in the oldest trading maxim: trade what you see, not what you think.

A year ago, I saw gold getting ready to move. Not because I had a bunker full of beans and bullion, but because the charts were whispering—and then shouting—that something was afoot. When markets speak, I listen. So, I started asking why gold was perking up.

Forget the fairy tale about governments someday going back to a gold standard. That ship sank with Nixon and isn’t coming back. However, central banks still hoard gold like it’s sacred. Why?

Here’s the answer that stuck: gold is the currency of war.

It has use cases, sure: electronics, jewellery, reserve diversification—but its strategic utility is what counts now. In a world of rising geopolitical strain, gold becomes the fallback. When the chips are down, it’s not crypto or fiat; it’s gold.

So, what are we seeing now?

Not Armageddon. But the pressure is rising. The ratchet is tightening. The system’s not breaking, but the creaks are getting louder.

Look at the United States. Its sudden industrial policy pivots aren’t random noise. The CHIPS Act, the push for domestic steel, the re-onshoring of manufacturing, the new buzz around shipbuilding—these aren’t isolated quirks. When you connect the dots, a pattern emerges: the U.S. is quietly (or not so quietly) positioning itself for potential conflict with China.

Now, that doesn’t mean war is imminent. Deterrence is a posture, not a plan. The best way to avoid war, historically, has been to be ready for it. That’s what we may be seeing: the U.S. gearing up not to fight, but to be taken seriously if it has to be.

That means rebuilding industrial muscle—because you can’t win a 21st-century conflict with PowerPoint presentations and imported semiconductors. You need steel, ships, chips, AI, and energy independence. Right now, America is strong militarily but hollowed out industrially. That’s changing.

This isn’t fate. It’s a thesis. And it’s tradable.

Because if this trend continues—if tension ratchets further—markets will reflect it long before the headlines do. That’s where gold comes in. It’s not about apocalypse. It’s about hedging tension.

And it’s not just gold. Rising tension affects everything. Some sectors thrive. Others get crushed.

Likely Outperformers:

  • Defence
  • Steel
  • Oil and Energy
  • Shipping
  • Chemicals
  • Agriculture and Food

Likely Underperformers:

  • Luxury goods
  • Travel
  • Consumer discretionary
  • Real estate
  • Entertainment

Think in pairs and hedge accordingly.

Take LVMH. I said in my article of March 26th that it looked like a canary in the coal mine—high on the globalisation pyramid, vulnerable to tariffs, recession, and geopolitical heat. Here’s the chart from then.

Here’s the chart now.

Uncomfortable accuracy.

The worst part? U.S. markets are still priced for perfection. Valuations remain near historic highs. If we do get turbulence, the drop could be sharp and long.

Gold, on the other hand, is already calling it. It’s telling us the mood music has changed. This doesn’t mean disaster is baked in. There are plenty of off-ramps. If things de-escalate, gold will drop—and I’ll be glad. Peace is more profitable than conflict.

But for now, I’m trading what I see, and what I see is a market that’s beginning to price in an increasingly dangerous world.
